title: Learning Rate Decay
concept: Learning Rate Decay gradually shrinks the step size during training to explore quickly at first and settle precisely at the bottom.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is Mini-Batch Gradient Descent the modern standard for training AI models?

options.text: It combines the speed of SGD with the stability of Batch GD by using small groups of data.
It ignores the cost function completely to save time.
It only works when the computer is offline.

exercises.explain: Correct! Mini-batching is the sweet spot. Now, how do we make sure our steps aren't too big when we get close to the target?

cards.title: Parking a car
cards.body: When driving home, you speed down the highway. But as you pull into your garage, you slow to a crawl. If you kept your highway speed, you'd crash into the wall!

cards.title: Learning Rate Decay
cards.body: Learning Rate Decay is the technique of gradually lowering the step size (learning rate) as training progresses over time.

cards.title: Shaking the board
cards.body: Imagine a ball in a bowl. Big shakes (high learning rate) help the ball roll out of small bumps, but keep it bouncing wildly. Shaking more gently (decay) lets it settle right at the bottom.

cards.title: Epoch by epoch
cards.body: At epoch 1 (first pass), the learning rate is 0.1. By epoch 50, it decays to 0.01. By epoch 100, it's 0.001, allowing the model to make micro-adjustments.

cards.title: Don't decay too fast
cards.body: If you shrink the learning rate too early, the model stops moving before it ever reaches the valley. Decay must be balanced: fast enough to settle, slow enough to arrive.

cards.title: Fixed vs. Decaying Rate
cards.body: How step sizes behave as the model gets close to the bottom:

right.text:
Big steps early
Tiny, precise steps late

exercises.prompt: Why is it useful to start training with a large learning rate?

options.text: To traverse the loss landscape quickly and escape poor initial zones.
To prevent the computer from overheating.
Because large steps are always more accurate.
To save GPU memory during the first steps.

exercises.explain: Yes! Large steps early on help the optimizer make rapid progress and avoid getting stuck in small, shallow local dips.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If the learning rate never decays, the model might bounce back and forth across the minimum without ever settling.
exercises.explain: True! A fixed learning rate that is too large will overshoot the valley floor and keep orbiting it instead of stopping.

exercises.sentence: Learning rate decay gradually ___ the step size as training time increases.
exercises.explain: Correct! Shrunk step sizes let the optimizer perform fine-grained optimization as it nears the lowest error.

exercises.prompt: What happens if the learning rate decays too quickly?

options.text: The steps become too tiny to reach the minimum, stopping progress prematurely.
The model will train infinitely fast.
The error automatically drops to zero.
The learning rate will double in value.

exercises.explain: Exactly! If decay is too aggressive, the steps become microscopic before the model gets close to the bottom.

exercises.prompt: Your loss graph shows the error dropping quickly at first, but then it flattens out and bounces up and down in a wide band near the bottom. What should you do?

options.text: Implement learning rate decay to shrink the steps so the optimizer can settle into the bottom of the valley.
Increase the learning rate to make the bounces even larger.
Delete half of your training dataset to force a straight path.
Stop training because a bouncing error means the model is broken.

exercises.explain: Brilliant! Shrinking the learning rate behaves like bringing a car to a gentle halt right at its parking spot. Next: how to use physical momentum to roll over bumps!
